name: Ytterbium
name_origin: Named after the village of Ytterby in Sweden, source of several rare-earth minerals.

occurrence.abundance_summary: Moderately abundant lanthanide (~3.2 ppm in crust); recovered chiefly from monazite, xenotime, and ion-adsorption clays.

compounds.name: Ytterbium(III) oxide
compounds.summary: White solid used as a precursor for ytterbium-doped laser hosts and high-temperature ceramics.

compounds.name: Ytterbium(III) chloride
compounds.summary: White hygroscopic salt; precursor for ytterbium metal and Lewis acid catalyst in some organic reactions.

compounds.name: Ytterbium(III) fluoride
compounds.summary: Used in dental cements (radio-opacifier) and as a feedstock for ytterbium metal production.

compounds.name: Ytterbium-doped yttrium aluminium garnet
compounds.summary: Active medium for high-power ~1.03 micrometre solid-state and fibre lasers, including disk lasers.

uses: Yb-doped fibre and disk lasers for industrial cutting, welding, and marking
Yb-171 atomic clocks with extraordinary frequency stability
Dopant for Yb:YAG and Yb:KGW solid-state lasers used in scientific research
Stress-strain gauges that exploit ytterbium's pressure-induced resistivity change
Doping in stainless steel to improve grain refinement and mechanical properties
reactivity_summary: Soft, silvery, ductile lanthanide that is unusual in being divalent in some compounds; tarnishes slowly in dry air, oxidises rapidly in moist air. Reacts with water (slowly cold, faster hot) and dissolves readily in dilute acids; the metal can ignite in air at elevated temperatures.
isotopes_summary: Ytterbium has seven naturally occurring stable isotopes (Yb-168, 170, 171, 172, 173, 174, 176). Yb-174 is the most abundant (~31.83%). Yb-169 (half-life 32 days) is used as a portable X-ray source and gamma-ray standard.

citations.source: Royal Society of Chemistry - Periodic Table: Ytterbium
IUPAC Periodic Table of the Elements (2021)
CRC Handbook of Chemistry and Physics, 102nd Edition
